pillar.metaTitle: Nursing Homes Near Me: Costs & Care | 1-800-MEDIGAP
pillar.metaDescription: Find nursing homes near you, compare costs, ratings, and how to pay with Medicare or Medicaid. Free senior care guidance — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
pillar.h1: Nursing Homes Near Me: Costs, Ratings & How to Pay
pillar.quickAnswer: Nursing homes provide 24-hour custodial and skilled care for seniors who can no longer live safely alone. The median cost is about $9,277 a month for a semi-private room (Genworth, 2024). Medicare covers only short-term skilled stays; long-term care is paid by Medicaid, long-term care insurance, or private funds. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help.
pillar.heroSubhead: Choosing a nursing home is one of the hardest decisions a family makes. Compare nearby facilities, understand real costs, check Medicare star ratings, and learn how to pay — with free, no-pressure guidance from 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
pillar.intro: Finding the right nursing home near you means balancing quality, location, and cost. This guide explains what nursing homes cost in 2026, how to compare ratings, and every way to pay — including Medicare, Medicaid, and long-term care insurance.

sections.h2: What is a nursing home and who needs one?
sections.body: A nursing home is a residential facility providing 24-hour care for seniors who cannot manage daily activities — bathing, dressing, eating, or medication — safely on their own. Unlike assisted living, nursing homes offer skilled medical supervision around the clock, with licensed nurses on staff. They serve people recovering from surgery, managing chronic conditions like dementia or Parkinson's, or needing long-term custodial care. Roughly 1.2 million Americans live in certified nursing homes (CMS, 2024). If your loved one needs more help than family or in-home care can provide, a nursing home may be the safest option. Our agents can help you weigh alternatives at no cost — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: How much do nursing homes cost near me in 2026?
sections.body: Nursing home costs are among the highest in senior care. The 2024 median was $9,277 per month for a semi-private room and $10,646 for a private room (Genworth Cost of Care Survey), and prices have risen since. Costs vary sharply by state — Alaska and the Northeast run far above the national median, while parts of the South cost less. Location, room type, staffing levels, and care needs all affect price. Because few families can pay $100,000-plus a year out of pocket, planning how to fund care is essential. A 1-800-MEDIGAP specialist can explain your payment options free of charge.

sections.h2: How do I compare nursing home ratings and quality?
sections.body: Medicare's Care Compare tool (medicare.gov/care-compare) rates every certified nursing home from 1 to 5 stars based on health inspections, staffing levels, and quality measures. A 5-star facility ranks in the top tier for its state. Beyond stars, check the most recent inspection report, registered-nurse hours per resident, and turnover rates. Visit in person, talk to current residents' families, and observe meals and activities. Ratings are a starting point, not the whole story — a 4-star home near family may serve your loved one better than a distant 5-star. We can help you read the data; call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: Who pays for nursing home care?
sections.body: Four sources cover nursing home care. Medicare pays only for short-term skilled nursing after a qualifying hospital stay — up to 100 days, not long-term custodial care. Medicaid is the largest payer of long-term nursing home care nationally, covering low-income seniors who meet asset and income limits. Long-term care insurance, if purchased earlier, can cover daily costs. Private pay — savings, pensions, home equity — fills the gap for everyone else. Many families combine sources or use Medicaid spend-down strategies. Understanding which applies to your situation can save tens of thousands of dollars. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free guidance.

sections.h2: Nursing home vs. skilled nursing facility — what's the difference?
sections.body: The terms overlap but differ in purpose. A skilled nursing facility (SNF) provides short-term, medically intensive care — physical therapy, wound care, IV medication — usually after a hospital stay, and is often covered by Medicare. A nursing home provides long-term custodial care for residents who live there indefinitely. Many facilities are dual-certified and do both, which is why people use the names interchangeably. The key distinction matters for payment: Medicare covers skilled care; it does not cover long-term custodial care. Knowing which level your loved one qualifies for affects your costs significantly. Our agents can clarify your options at 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: How much does a nursing home cost per month?
faqs.a: The 2024 median nursing home cost was about $9,277 per month for a semi-private room and $10,646 for a private room, per the Genworth Cost of Care Survey. Actual prices vary widely by state, room type, and care needs, often exceeding $100,000 a year. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free payment guidance.

faqs.q: Does Medicare pay for nursing home care?
faqs.a: Medicare pays only for short-term skilled nursing care — up to 100 days after a qualifying three-day hospital stay. It does not cover long-term custodial care, which is the most common reason people enter nursing homes. Medicaid, long-term care insurance, or private funds cover long-term stays. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to understand your options.

faqs.q: Does Medicaid cover nursing home care?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicaid is the largest payer of long-term nursing home care in the U.S., covering low-income seniors who meet their state's income and asset limits (typically about $2,000 in countable assets for an individual). Eligibility rules and spend-down strategies vary by state. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help understanding qualification.

faqs.q: How do I find the best nursing home near me?
faqs.a: Start with Medicare's Care Compare tool to check star ratings, then visit facilities in person, review recent inspection reports, and ask about staffing levels and turnover. Prioritize proximity to family. A nearby, well-staffed 4-star home often serves residents better than a distant 5-star. Our agents can help you compare — call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a nursing home and a skilled nursing facility?
faqs.a: A skilled nursing facility provides short-term, medically intensive care (often Medicare-covered) after a hospital stay. A nursing home provides long-term custodial care for residents who live there indefinitely. Many facilities do both. The distinction matters mainly for payment, since Medicare covers skilled care but not long-term custodial care.

faqs.q: How can I pay for a nursing home if I run out of money?
faqs.a: If savings run out, Medicaid becomes the primary payer for nursing home care once you meet income and asset limits. Many families use a legal Medicaid spend-down to qualify. Long-term care insurance and veterans benefits may also help. Planning early protects assets — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free guidance.

faqs.q: How are nursing homes rated?
faqs.a: Medicare's Care Compare rates certified nursing homes from 1 to 5 stars using three measures: health inspection results, staffing levels (especially registered-nurse hours), and clinical quality measures. The ratings are updated regularly at medicare.gov/care-compare. Use them alongside in-person visits and inspection reports for a full picture.

faqs.q: Can 1-800-MEDIGAP help me choose and pay for a nursing home?
faqs.a: Yes. 1-800-MEDIGAP offers free, no-pressure guidance on comparing nursing homes, understanding Medicare and Medicaid coverage, and planning how to pay for long-term care. As the trusted toll-free number for senior needs, one call connects you to help. Dial 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

page.intro: Finding a trustworthy nursing home near you starts with the right tools and the right questions. Here's how to locate, compare, and choose local facilities.

sections.h2: How do I find nursing homes near me?
sections.body: The fastest way to find nursing homes near you is Medicare's Care Compare tool at medicare.gov/care-compare. Enter your ZIP code to see every Medicare- and Medicaid-certified facility within your area, each with a 1-to-5-star overall rating, staffing data, and inspection history. Your local Area Agency on Aging and hospital discharge planners can also recommend facilities. Once you have a shortlist, visit in person — quality is best judged by seeing meals, staff interactions, and cleanliness firsthand. Proximity to family matters too, since frequent visits improve resident outcomes. Need help narrowing the list? Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: What should I look for in a local nursing home?
sections.body: Beyond star ratings, focus on staffing levels — especially registered-nurse hours per resident — and staff turnover, which strongly predict care quality. Read the most recent state inspection report for any serious deficiencies. Ask how the facility handles emergencies, what activities are offered, and whether your loved one's doctor can stay involved. Confirm whether the home accepts Medicaid, in case private funds run out later. Trust your instincts during visits; a welcoming, well-staffed home near family often outperforms a distant top-rated one. Our specialists can help you evaluate options free of charge at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: How do I find a nursing home near me?
faqs.a: Use Medicare's Care Compare tool at medicare.gov/care-compare and enter your ZIP code to see every certified facility nearby with star ratings, staffing data, and inspection records. Your local Area Agency on Aging and hospital discharge planners can also help. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free guidance.

faqs.q: How much do nursing homes near me cost?
faqs.a: The national median was about $9,277 per month for a semi-private room in 2024 (Genworth), but local prices vary widely by state and facility. Costs depend on room type, staffing, and care needs. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn how Medicare, Medicaid, or insurance may help pay.

faqs.q: Which nursing homes near me accept Medicaid?
faqs.a: Most, but not all, nursing homes accept Medicaid. Care Compare lets you filter for Medicaid-certified facilities, and you can confirm directly with each home. Accepting Medicaid matters if private funds may run out during a long stay. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for help.

faqs.q: Can 1-800-MEDIGAP help me find a nursing home?
faqs.a: Yes. 1-800-MEDIGAP provides free, no-pressure help comparing nearby nursing homes, understanding ratings, and figuring out how to pay through Medicare or Medicaid. One call connects you to a specialist. Dial 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

Always tour in person before deciding. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help comparing top local facilities.
page.heroSubhead: Want the highest-quality nursing home nearby? Learn how to identify the best-rated, best-staffed facilities — with free guidance from 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: The best nursing home isn't just the highest-rated — it's the one that fits your loved one's needs, budget, and location. Here's how to identify it.

sections.h2: How do I identify the best nursing homes near me?
sections.body: Start with Medicare's Care Compare star ratings, which combine health inspections, staffing, and quality measures into a 1-to-5 score. The best facilities earn 4 or 5 stars and show high registered-nurse hours per resident and low staff turnover — two of the strongest predictors of good care. Read the most recent inspection report for any abuse, neglect, or safety citations. Then tour your top choices in person: watch staff interact with residents, observe a meal, and ask current families about their experience. The best home balances quality with proximity to family. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help shortlisting.

sections.h2: Are 5-star nursing homes always the best choice?
sections.body: Not necessarily. A 5-star rating signals strong measured quality, but ratings have limits — they rely partly on self-reported data and may not capture the day-to-day feel of a home. A nearby 4-star facility where family can visit often, with staff who know your loved one by name, may deliver better real-world outcomes than a distant 5-star home. Specialized care also matters: the best dementia or rehabilitation unit may not have the highest overall star count. Use ratings to narrow your list, then let in-person visits and care-specific needs guide the final choice. Our agents can help you weigh trade-offs free at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: What makes a nursing home one of the best?
faqs.a: The best nursing homes combine high Medicare star ratings (4-5 stars), strong registered-nurse staffing, low staff turnover, clean inspection reports, and a welcoming environment — located close enough for regular family visits. Specialized care for conditions like dementia also matters. Tour in person before deciding. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: How do I check nursing home star ratings?
faqs.a: Visit Medicare's Care Compare at medicare.gov/care-compare and enter your ZIP code. Each certified facility shows a 1-to-5-star overall rating plus separate scores for health inspections, staffing, and quality measures. Use these to build a shortlist, then verify with in-person visits.

faqs.q: Is a 5-star nursing home always better than a 4-star?
faqs.a: Not always. Ratings rely partly on self-reported data and may miss day-to-day quality. A nearby 4-star home with frequent family visits and staff who know your loved one can outperform a distant 5-star facility. Use ratings to narrow choices, then tour in person. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help.

faqs.q: Can 1-800-MEDIGAP recommend the best nursing homes near me?
faqs.a: 1-800-MEDIGAP provides free, no-pressure guidance on comparing top-rated local nursing homes, reading Medicare ratings, and understanding costs and payment. As the trusted toll-free number for senior needs, one call connects you to help. Dial 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

page.h1: How Much Does a Nursing Home Cost?
page.quickAnswer: A nursing home costs about $9,277 per month for a semi-private room and $10,646 for a private room nationally (Genworth Cost of Care Survey, 2024) — roughly $111,000 to $128,000 a year. Prices vary widely by state and care needs. Medicare covers only short-term skilled stays. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free payment guidance.

page.intro: Nursing home care is among the most expensive senior services in America. Here's what it costs in 2026 and what makes the price vary.

sections.h2: What does a nursing home cost in 2026?
sections.body: The most recent national benchmark, the Genworth 2024 Cost of Care Survey, put the median nursing home cost at $9,277 per month for a semi-private room and $10,646 for a private room — about $111,000 to $128,000 per year. Costs have continued rising with inflation and staffing pressures. State variation is dramatic: Alaska, the Northeast, and parts of the West Coast run well above the median, while several Southern and Midwestern states fall below it. These figures cover room, board, nursing care, and basic services, but extras like specialized therapy or a private room raise the total. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to learn how to cover these costs.

sections.h2: What factors affect nursing home cost?
sections.body: Several factors drive nursing home pricing. Room type is the biggest single variable — private rooms cost more than semi-private. Location matters enormously, with high-cost-of-living states charging two to three times more than the cheapest regions. Level of care also affects price: residents needing memory care, intensive therapy, or one-on-one supervision pay more. Facility quality and amenities, staffing ratios, and whether the home is for-profit or nonprofit all play a role. Because costs add up fast, planning how to pay — through Medicaid, long-term care insurance, or private funds — is critical. Our specialists explain your options free at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: How much does a nursing home cost per year?
faqs.a: At the 2024 national median, a semi-private nursing home room costs about $111,000 per year and a private room about $128,000 (Genworth). Actual costs vary widely by state and care needs, and prices have risen since. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to learn how to pay.

faqs.q: Why are nursing homes so expensive?
faqs.a: Nursing homes provide 24-hour skilled care, room, board, meals, and supervision, requiring large staffs of nurses and aides. Staffing costs, regulatory requirements, and rising labor and real-estate prices all drive cost. Private rooms and specialized care like memory units add more. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for payment help.

faqs.q: Does the cost of a nursing home vary by state?
faqs.a: Yes, dramatically. High-cost states like Alaska and those in the Northeast can run two to three times the price of the least expensive Southern and Midwestern states. Always check local rates for facilities near you. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free guidance.

faqs.q: How can I afford a nursing home?
faqs.a: Most families pay through a combination of Medicaid (for those who qualify), long-term care insurance, veterans benefits, and private funds like savings or home equity. Medicare only covers short-term skilled stays. Planning early protects assets. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free guidance.

page.heroSubhead: Understand the national and state averages for nursing home care, and learn how to pay — with free guidance from 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: Knowing the average cost of nursing home care helps families plan ahead. Here are the latest national and state-level numbers.

sections.h2: What is the average cost of nursing home care?
sections.body: Nationally, the average (median) nursing home cost was $9,277 per month for a semi-private room and $10,646 for a private room in 2024, according to the Genworth Cost of Care Survey — roughly $111,000 to $128,000 a year. These averages have risen with inflation and staffing shortages. It's important to understand that 'average' masks wide variation: your local cost could be substantially higher or lower depending on state and facility. The figure covers room, board, nursing care, and basic services. Because almost no family can sustain six-figure annual costs indefinitely, knowing your payment options early is essential. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help.

sections.h2: How do averages differ by state and room type?
sections.body: State averages range enormously. The Northeast, Alaska, and the West Coast typically post the highest figures, while many Southern and Midwestern states fall well below the national median. Room type also shifts the average: a private room runs roughly $1,000-plus more per month than semi-private. Care level matters too — memory care and high-acuity nursing raise the average bill. When budgeting, use your specific state and city numbers rather than the national figure, since the gap can exceed several thousand dollars a month. A 1-800-MEDIGAP specialist can help you understand local costs and how Medicaid or insurance can offset them, at no charge.

faqs.q: What is the average monthly cost of nursing home care?
faqs.a: The 2024 national average was $9,277 per month for a semi-private room and $10,646 for a private room (Genworth Cost of Care Survey). These figures vary widely by state and have risen since. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to learn how to pay.

faqs.q: Is the average cost of nursing home care rising?
faqs.a: Yes. Nursing home costs have climbed steadily with inflation, labor shortages, and higher staffing requirements. The 2024 Genworth median of $9,277 a month for a semi-private room is higher than prior years, and increases are expected to continue. Plan ahead — call 1-800-MEDIGAP for guidance.

faqs.q: Why is the average so different from my local cost?
faqs.a: National averages blend high- and low-cost states. The Northeast and Alaska run far above the median, while parts of the South and Midwest fall below it. Always check rates for facilities in your specific area rather than relying on the national figure. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover the average cost of nursing home care?
faqs.a: No. Medicare covers only short-term skilled nursing care (up to 100 days after a qualifying hospital stay), not the long-term custodial care most nursing home residents need. Long-term costs are paid by Medicaid, insurance, or private funds. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for help.

page.quickAnswer: Nursing home cost per month averages $9,277 for a semi-private room and $10,646 for a private room nationally (Genworth, 2024). Monthly prices vary by state, room type, and care level, and many states run well above average. Medicare doesn't cover long-term stays. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free payment help.
page.heroSubhead: Budgeting for monthly nursing home costs? Learn what's included and how to cover it — with free help from 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: Nursing home costs are usually quoted per month or per day. Here's what a monthly stay costs and what that price covers.

sections.h2: What is the monthly cost of a nursing home?
sections.body: The national median monthly nursing home cost in 2024 was $9,277 for a semi-private room and $10,646 for a private room (Genworth Cost of Care Survey). Translated to a daily rate, that's roughly $305 to $350 per day. Monthly prices climb in high-cost states and for residents needing memory care or intensive nursing. The monthly figure typically includes room, board, meals, 24-hour nursing supervision, housekeeping, and basic personal care. Extras like specialized therapy, a private room, salon services, or certain medications may be billed separately. Because the monthly cost rarely drops, planning a sustainable funding source is vital. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free guidance.

sections.h2: What's included in the monthly nursing home price?
sections.body: A standard monthly nursing home rate generally covers your room, all meals and snacks, 24-hour nursing care, help with daily activities like bathing and dressing, housekeeping and laundry, and basic recreational activities. What's often not included: physician visits, prescription drugs, physical or occupational therapy beyond a covered period, private-duty aides, and personal extras. Ask each facility for an itemized list of what the base rate covers and what costs more, so you can compare apples to apples. Understanding the full monthly bill prevents surprises. Our agents can help you compare facility pricing and find ways to pay, free of charge, at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: How much is a nursing home per month?
faqs.a: The 2024 national median was $9,277 per month for a semi-private room and $10,646 for a private room (Genworth). That's roughly $305-$350 per day. Monthly costs vary widely by state and care needs. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to learn how to pay.

faqs.q: What does the monthly nursing home cost include?
faqs.a: The base monthly rate usually covers your room, meals, 24-hour nursing care, help with daily activities, housekeeping, and basic activities. Physician visits, prescriptions, specialized therapy, and private aides often cost extra. Ask each facility for an itemized list. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help comparing.

faqs.q: How is nursing home cost calculated — monthly or daily?
faqs.a: Facilities typically quote both a daily and a monthly rate. At the 2024 median of $9,277 a month for a semi-private room, the daily rate is about $305. Higher care levels and private rooms raise the figure. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for guidance.

faqs.q: Can Medicaid cover the monthly nursing home cost?
faqs.a: Yes, for those who qualify. Medicaid is the largest payer of long-term nursing home care and can cover the full monthly cost for low-income seniors who meet income and asset limits. Residents typically contribute most of their income toward care. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to check eligibility.

page.h1: How to Pay for Nursing Home Care
page.quickAnswer: You can pay for nursing home care through four main sources: Medicaid (the largest payer of long-term care, for those who qualify financially), long-term care insurance, veterans benefits, and private funds like savings or home equity. Medicare covers only short-term skilled stays. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free planning help.
page.heroSubhead: Nursing home care can cost over $100,000 a year. Learn every way to pay and protect your assets — with free guidance from 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: With nursing home costs exceeding six figures annually, knowing how to pay is critical. Here are your main funding options.

sections.h2: What are the main ways to pay for nursing home care?
sections.body: Four sources cover nursing home care. Medicaid is the largest payer of long-term custodial care nationally, covering low-income seniors who meet income and asset limits — many families use legal spend-down strategies to qualify. Long-term care insurance, if purchased before care is needed, pays a daily or monthly benefit. Veterans and surviving spouses may qualify for VA Aid and Attendance benefits. Private pay — savings, pensions, Social Security, and home equity (sometimes via a reverse mortgage or annuity) — covers the rest. Most families combine sources over time as savings deplete and Medicaid takes over. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help mapping your options.

sections.h2: Does Medicare help pay for nursing home care?
sections.body: Medicare's role is limited. It pays only for short-term skilled nursing care — up to 100 days following a qualifying three-day inpatient hospital stay, with full coverage for days 1-20 and a daily coinsurance for days 21-100. Medicare does not pay for long-term custodial care, which is what most nursing home residents need. A Medicare Supplement (Medigap) plan can cover the day 21-100 skilled-nursing coinsurance, easing that short-term cost. For the long-term stay itself, you'll rely on Medicaid, insurance, or private funds. Understanding this gap early prevents costly surprises. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn how Medigap and other coverage fit together.

faqs.q: How do most people pay for nursing home care?
faqs.a: Most people pay through a combination of Medicaid (the largest payer of long-term care), long-term care insurance, veterans benefits, and private funds like savings and home equity. Many families start with private pay and transition to Medicaid once assets are spent down. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for help.

faqs.q: Does Medicare pay for long-term nursing home care?
faqs.a: No. Medicare covers only short-term skilled nursing care — up to 100 days after a qualifying hospital stay — not long-term custodial care. A Medigap plan can cover the day 21-100 skilled coinsurance, but long-term stays are paid by Medicaid, insurance, or private funds. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: Can I use insurance to pay for a nursing home?
faqs.a: Yes, if you bought long-term care insurance before needing care; it pays a daily or monthly benefit toward nursing home costs. Some life insurance policies offer long-term care riders. Standard health insurance and Medicare do not cover long-term stays. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for guidance.

faqs.q: Are there veterans benefits for nursing home care?
faqs.a: Yes. Wartime veterans and surviving spouses who meet service and financial criteria may qualify for VA Aid and Attendance benefits, which help pay for nursing home and long-term care. The VA also operates community living centers. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to explore your options.

faqs.q: How can 1-800-MEDIGAP help me pay for nursing home care?
faqs.a: 1-800-MEDIGAP offers free, no-pressure guidance on Medicaid eligibility, long-term care insurance, veterans benefits, and how Medicare and Medigap fit in. One call connects you to a specialist who can map your options. Dial 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

page.metaTitle: Does Medicaid Cover Nursing Home Care? | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Yes — Medicaid is the largest payer of long-term nursing home care for those who meet income and asset limits. See how to qualify. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: Does Medicaid Cover Nursing Home Care?
page.quickAnswer: Yes. Medicaid is the largest payer of long-term nursing home care in the U.S., covering room, board, and care for low-income seniors who meet their state's income and asset limits — typically about $2,000 in countable assets for an individual. Residents contribute most of their income toward care. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free eligibility help.
page.heroSubhead: Medicaid pays for most long-term nursing home stays in America. Learn whether you qualify — with free guidance from 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: Unlike Medicare, Medicaid covers long-term nursing home care. Here's what it pays for and who qualifies.

sections.h2: What does Medicaid cover in a nursing home?
sections.body: For eligible seniors, Medicaid covers the full cost of nursing home care — room and board, nursing services, personal care, meals, and most medications — in a Medicaid-certified facility. It is the nation's primary payer of long-term custodial care, financing care for the majority of nursing home residents. In exchange, residents generally must contribute nearly all of their monthly income (Social Security, pensions) toward the cost, keeping only a small personal-needs allowance set by their state. Medicaid then pays the remaining balance directly to the facility. Because Medicaid is administered by states, exact rules and covered services vary. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to understand your state's coverage.

sections.h2: Who qualifies for Medicaid nursing home coverage?
sections.body: Medicaid nursing home eligibility has two parts: financial and medical. Financially, you must meet your state's income limit and asset limit — typically around $2,000 in countable assets for an individual, though a home, one vehicle, and certain other assets are usually exempt. Spouses of nursing home residents are protected by 'spousal impoverishment' rules that let the community spouse keep a portion of assets and income. Medically, you must require a nursing-home level of care. Many families legally 'spend down' excess assets to qualify. A five-year look-back period reviews asset transfers, so early planning matters. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free help navigating qualification.

faqs.q: Does Medicaid cover nursing home care?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicaid is the largest payer of long-term nursing home care in the U.S. For eligible low-income seniors, it covers room, board, nursing services, and care in a Medicaid-certified facility. Residents contribute most of their income toward the cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to check eligibility.

faqs.q: What are the asset limits for Medicaid nursing home coverage?
faqs.a: Most states limit countable assets to about $2,000 for an individual, though a primary home (up to an equity cap), one vehicle, and certain other assets are usually exempt. Spousal impoverishment rules protect the at-home spouse. Limits vary by state. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for help.

faqs.q: Does Medicaid pay the full nursing home cost?
faqs.a: For eligible residents, Medicaid pays the full cost of care in a certified facility, but residents must contribute nearly all their monthly income (Social Security, pensions), keeping only a small personal-needs allowance. Medicaid covers the remaining balance. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn your state's rules.

faqs.q: Can I qualify for Medicaid if I own a home?
faqs.a: Often yes. A primary residence is usually exempt from Medicaid's asset limit up to an equity cap, especially if a spouse or dependent lives there. However, estate recovery may later seek repayment from the home. Rules vary by state. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for guidance.

faqs.q: How can 1-800-MEDIGAP help with Medicaid coverage?
faqs.a: 1-800-MEDIGAP offers free, no-pressure guidance on Medicaid eligibility, asset limits, spend-down, and how it works with Medicare. As the trusted toll-free number for senior needs, one call connects you to help. Dial 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

page.metaDescription: Understand Medicare's 5-star nursing home ratings — inspections, staffing, and quality measures. Compare facilities free. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: Medicare rates every certified nursing home from 1 to 5 stars on medicare.gov/care-compare, based on three measures: health inspection results, staffing levels, and clinical quality measures. Five stars is the top tier; one star is well below average. Use ratings alongside in-person visits. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help comparing facilities.
page.heroSubhead: Star ratings make comparing nursing homes easier — but only if you know what they measure. Get free help interpreting them from 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: Medicare's 5-star rating system is the most trusted way to compare nursing home quality. Here's how it works and how to use it.

sections.h2: How does Medicare's nursing home rating system work?
sections.body: Medicare's Care Compare assigns each certified nursing home an overall rating from 1 to 5 stars, built from three components. Health inspections reflect findings from state surveys over the past three years, weighted toward recent and serious deficiencies. Staffing measures registered-nurse and total nurse hours per resident per day, since staffing strongly predicts quality. Quality measures track clinical outcomes like falls, pressure ulcers, and hospital readmissions. Five stars means 'much above average' for the state; one star means 'much below average.' The ratings are updated regularly and freely available at medicare.gov/care-compare. They're an excellent starting point. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for help reading them.

sections.h2: How should I use nursing home ratings when choosing?
sections.body: Use star ratings to build a shortlist, not to make the final decision alone. Pay special attention to the staffing and health-inspection sub-ratings, which are harder to game than self-reported quality measures. Then dig deeper: read the full inspection report for any abuse, neglect, or safety citations, and note whether problems were recent or resolved. Finally, visit in person — ratings can't capture cleanliness, staff warmth, or how residents are treated day to day. Combine the data with what you see and hear from current families. Our specialists can help you interpret ratings and reports at no cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: How are nursing homes rated?
faqs.a: Medicare's Care Compare rates certified nursing homes from 1 to 5 stars based on three measures: health inspection results, staffing levels (especially registered-nurse hours), and clinical quality measures like falls and readmissions. Ratings are updated regularly at medicare.gov/care-compare. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for help.

faqs.q: What does a 5-star nursing home rating mean?
faqs.a: A 5-star rating means the facility ranks 'much above average' for its state on Medicare's combined measures of inspections, staffing, and quality. One star means 'much below average.' Ratings are a strong starting point but should be paired with in-person visits and inspection reports. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: Where can I check nursing home ratings?
faqs.a: Check official ratings at Medicare's Care Compare tool, medicare.gov/care-compare. Enter a ZIP code to see every certified facility's overall star rating plus separate scores for inspections, staffing, and quality measures, along with recent inspection reports. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for help interpreting them.

faqs.q: Are nursing home ratings reliable?
faqs.a: Medicare's ratings are a useful, government-verified benchmark, but they have limits — some quality data is self-reported. The inspection and staffing sub-ratings are the most reliable. Always confirm with in-person visits and full inspection reports before deciding. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for guidance.

page.metaTitle: How to Choose a Nursing Home | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Learn how to choose a nursing home — ratings, staffing, inspections, cost, and what to look for on a tour. Free help comparing. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: How to Choose a Nursing Home
page.quickAnswer: To choose a nursing home, start with Medicare's star ratings on Care Compare, then check staffing levels and recent inspection reports, confirm it accepts your payment source (including Medicaid), and visit in person to observe care, cleanliness, and staff. Prioritize proximity to family. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help comparing options.
page.heroSubhead: Choosing a nursing home is a major decision. Follow a clear checklist and get free, no-pressure help from 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: The right nursing home keeps your loved one safe, comfortable, and well cared for. Here's a step-by-step approach to choosing wisely.

sections.h2: What steps should I follow to choose a nursing home?
sections.body: Start by listing facilities near family using Medicare's Care Compare tool, filtering for the level of care your loved one needs. Compare star ratings, focusing on staffing and inspection sub-scores. Confirm each home accepts your payment method — Medicare for short skilled stays, or Medicaid if long-term funds may run out. Next, tour your top choices in person on different days and times. Observe staff-to-resident interactions, cleanliness, meal quality, and resident engagement. Ask about staff turnover, nurse coverage, and how medical needs are handled. Finally, talk to current residents' families. Weigh quality against location. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help at every step.

sections.h2: What questions should I ask on a nursing home tour?
sections.body: On a tour, ask the questions ratings can't answer. How many residents does each nurse and aide care for, and does that change at night? What is the staff turnover rate? How are medical emergencies handled, and is a physician on call? Can residents keep their own doctor? What activities, therapies, and dietary options are offered? How does the home prevent and report falls, infections, and pressure sores? What happens if private funds run out — does it accept Medicaid? Request a copy of the latest inspection report. Trust your senses: cleanliness, odors, and how residents look and are treated reveal a lot. Our agents can help you prepare. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: How do I choose the right nursing home?
faqs.a: Compare Medicare star ratings on Care Compare, check staffing and inspection reports, confirm the home accepts your payment source including Medicaid, and tour in person to observe care and cleanliness. Prioritize proximity to family and talk to current residents' families. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help.

faqs.q: What should I look for when touring a nursing home?
faqs.a: Watch staff-to-resident interactions, cleanliness, and meal quality. Ask about staffing ratios, turnover, nurse coverage at night, how emergencies are handled, and whether the home accepts Medicaid. Note odors, resident engagement, and overall atmosphere. Request the latest inspection report. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a tour checklist.

faqs.q: How important is location when choosing a nursing home?
faqs.a: Very important. Residents whose families visit often tend to receive better attention and have better outcomes. A nearby, well-staffed facility frequently serves your loved one better than a distant top-rated one. Balance quality ratings with proximity to family. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for guidance.

faqs.q: Should I choose a nursing home that accepts Medicaid?
faqs.a: If there's any chance private funds could run out during a long stay, yes — choosing a Medicaid-certified home avoids forcing a stressful move later. Confirm Medicaid acceptance before admission. Care Compare lets you filter for it. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for help.

faqs.q: Can 1-800-MEDIGAP help me choose a nursing home?
faqs.a: Yes. 1-800-MEDIGAP offers free, no-pressure help comparing nursing homes, reading ratings and inspection reports, and understanding payment options. One call connects you to a specialist. Dial 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

children.keyword: how to qualify for medicaid nursing home

page.metaTitle: How to Qualify for Medicaid Nursing Home | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Qualify for Medicaid nursing home coverage by meeting income, asset, and care-level rules. Learn spend-down and look-back facts. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: How to Qualify for Medicaid Nursing Home Coverage
page.quickAnswer: To qualify for Medicaid nursing home coverage, you must meet your state's income limit, an asset limit (typically about $2,000 in countable assets for an individual), and require a nursing-home level of care. A home, one vehicle, and certain assets are exempt. A 5-year look-back reviews asset transfers. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help.
page.heroSubhead: Qualifying for Medicaid can make long-term nursing home care affordable. Learn the rules and get free guidance from 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: Medicaid pays for most long-term nursing home care, but qualifying involves financial and medical rules. Here's how it works.

sections.h2: What are the requirements to qualify for Medicaid nursing home care?
sections.body: Qualifying has two sides: financial and functional. Financially, your income must fall under your state's limit, and countable assets must be at or below roughly $2,000 for an individual (limits differ by state). Exempt assets typically include your primary home up to an equity cap, one vehicle, personal belongings, and a prepaid burial plan. Functionally, you must need a nursing-home level of care, confirmed by a medical assessment showing you require help with daily activities or skilled supervision. Married couples get special protections so the at-home spouse isn't left impoverished. Because rules are state-specific and complex, expert help avoids costly mistakes. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free guidance.

sections.h2: What is the Medicaid look-back period?
sections.body: Medicaid uses a 60-month (5-year) look-back period to review asset transfers before your application. If you gave away assets or sold them below fair market value during that window — to relatives, for example — Medicaid may impose a penalty period during which it won't pay for your care. This rule discourages hiding assets to qualify. Legitimate planning, like spending down on care, home modifications, or certain trusts, is allowed when done correctly and early. Because mistakes can delay coverage for months, families often work with an elder-law attorney or a knowledgeable advisor. Our specialists can point you in the right direction at no cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: How do I qualify for Medicaid nursing home coverage?
faqs.a: You must meet your state's income limit, hold countable assets at or below about $2,000 for an individual, and require a nursing-home level of care confirmed by a medical assessment. A home, one vehicle, and certain assets are exempt. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help.

faqs.q: What is the Medicaid 5-year look-back period?
faqs.a: Medicaid reviews asset transfers made in the 60 months before your application. Gifts or below-market sales during that window can trigger a penalty period when Medicaid won't pay for care. Legitimate spend-down done early is allowed. Plan ahead — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for guidance.

faqs.q: What assets are exempt for Medicaid nursing home eligibility?
faqs.a: Commonly exempt assets include your primary home up to an equity cap (especially if a spouse lives there), one vehicle, personal belongings, household goods, and a prepaid irrevocable burial plan. Countable assets like cash and investments must usually fall below about $2,000. Rules vary by state. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: Can my spouse keep assets if I go on Medicaid?
faqs.a: Yes. 'Spousal impoverishment' protections let the community (at-home) spouse keep a share of joint assets and a minimum monthly income, so they aren't left destitute. The protected amounts are set annually and vary by state. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for help understanding your situation.

faqs.q: How can 1-800-MEDIGAP help me qualify for Medicaid?
faqs.a: 1-800-MEDIGAP offers free, no-pressure guidance on Medicaid income and asset rules, spend-down, the look-back period, and spousal protections, and can point you to elder-law resources. One call connects you to help. Dial 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

page.metaDescription: Medicare covers up to 100 days of skilled nursing care after a 3-day hospital stay — days 1-20 free, 21-100 with coinsurance. Learn the rules. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: Medicare covers up to 100 days of skilled nursing facility care per benefit period after a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay. Medicare pays fully for days 1-20; days 21-100 require a daily coinsurance ($209.50 in 2025). After 100 days, you pay all costs. A Medigap plan can cover the coinsurance. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.heroSubhead: Medicare's 100-day skilled nursing benefit has strict rules. Understand exactly what's covered — with free help from 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: Medicare's 100-day skilled nursing benefit helps with short-term recovery, but it's often misunderstood. Here's how it actually works.

sections.h2: How does Medicare's 100-day skilled nursing benefit work?
sections.body: After a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least three consecutive days (not counting the discharge day), Medicare Part A covers skilled nursing facility care for up to 100 days per benefit period. Medicare pays the full cost for days 1 through 20. For days 21 through 100, you owe a daily coinsurance — $209.50 per day in 2025. After day 100, Medicare pays nothing and you're responsible for all costs. Coverage requires that you need daily skilled care (like physical therapy or skilled nursing) that can only be provided in a facility. If you stop improving or no longer need skilled care, coverage can end before 100 days. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for help.

sections.h2: How can I cover the days 21-100 coinsurance?
sections.body: The daily coinsurance for days 21-100 adds up quickly — at $209.50 a day, the full stretch can exceed $16,000. A Medicare Supplement (Medigap) plan is the most common way to cover this. Most Medigap plans, including popular Plans G and N, pay the skilled nursing facility coinsurance in full, so you owe nothing for days 21-100. Medicare Advantage plans handle skilled nursing differently, often with their own daily copays. Knowing your coverage before you need it prevents a large surprise bill. A licensed agent can review your Medigap or Advantage coverage and explain exactly what you'd pay. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a free, no-pressure review.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover 100 days of skilled nursing?
faqs.a: Medicare covers up to 100 days of skilled nursing facility care per benefit period after a qualifying 3-day hospital stay. Days 1-20 are fully covered; days 21-100 require a daily coinsurance ($209.50 in 2025). After 100 days you pay all costs. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for help.

faqs.q: What is the skilled nursing coinsurance for days 21-100?
faqs.a: In 2025, Medicare charges a daily coinsurance of $209.50 for skilled nursing facility days 21 through 100. Over the full period that exceeds $16,000. Most Medigap plans cover this coinsurance entirely. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to review your coverage.

faqs.q: What qualifies you for Medicare's 100-day skilled nursing benefit?
faqs.a: You need a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least 3 consecutive days, then admission to a Medicare-certified skilled nursing facility for daily skilled care (like therapy or skilled nursing) related to that hospital stay. Observation stays don't count. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help confirming eligibility.

faqs.q: What happens after Medicare's 100 days run out?
faqs.a: After day 100 in a benefit period, Medicare pays nothing for skilled nursing care and you're responsible for all costs. At that point, long-term care is paid by Medicaid, long-term care insurance, or private funds. A new benefit period can reset the count later. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: Does Medigap cover the skilled nursing coinsurance?
faqs.a: Yes. Most Medicare Supplement (Medigap) plans, including Plans G and N, cover the skilled nursing facility coinsurance for days 21-100 in full, so you owe nothing for that cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for a free review of your plan.

page.metaDescription: Find Medicare-certified skilled nursing facilities near you for short-term rehab and recovery. Compare ratings and coverage. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: To find a skilled nursing facility (SNF) near you, use Medicare's Care Compare at medicare.gov/care-compare and filter by location. SNFs provide short-term, medically intensive care like physical therapy and wound care, often after a hospital stay, and are covered by Medicare for up to 100 days. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help.
page.heroSubhead: Need short-term skilled care or rehab nearby? Find and compare Medicare-certified facilities with free help from 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: A skilled nursing facility provides short-term medical care and rehabilitation, usually after a hospital stay. Here's how to find one near you.

sections.h2: How do I find a skilled nursing facility near me?
sections.body: Use Medicare's Care Compare tool at medicare.gov/care-compare, enter your ZIP code, and filter for nursing homes, which include skilled nursing facilities. Each listing shows star ratings, staffing, and inspection history. Because most skilled nursing stays follow a hospital admission, hospital discharge planners and case managers are valuable resources — they coordinate directly with nearby SNFs and know which have openings and good rehab outcomes. Confirm the facility is Medicare-certified so your skilled stay is covered, and check that it offers the specific therapy your recovery requires (physical, occupational, or speech). Proximity to family aids recovery and oversight. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help comparing options.

sections.h2: What should I check before entering a skilled nursing facility?
sections.body: Before choosing a skilled nursing facility, confirm it's Medicare-certified and that your stay qualifies — you'll generally need a 3-day inpatient hospital stay first. Verify the facility offers the rehabilitation you need and review its star rating, especially staffing and inspection scores. Ask about therapy hours per day, the rehab team's experience, and discharge-planning support to help you return home safely. Understand your costs: Medicare covers days 1-20 fully, with coinsurance for days 21-100 that Medigap can cover. Confirming coverage and quality upfront prevents surprises during a stressful recovery. Our licensed agents can review your Medicare coverage free of charge. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: How do I find a skilled nursing facility near me?
faqs.a: Use Medicare's Care Compare at medicare.gov/care-compare, enter your ZIP code, and review star ratings and inspection data. Hospital discharge planners can also recommend nearby Medicare-certified facilities, since most skilled stays follow a hospital admission. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help comparing.

faqs.q: What is a skilled nursing facility?
faqs.a: A skilled nursing facility (SNF) provides short-term, medically intensive care such as physical therapy, wound care, and IV medication, usually after a hospital stay. It differs from long-term nursing home care and is covered by Medicare for up to 100 days per benefit period. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for details.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover a skilled nursing facility near me?
faqs.a: Yes, if it's Medicare-certified and you had a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay. Medicare covers up to 100 days per benefit period — days 1-20 fully, days 21-100 with a daily coinsurance that most Medigap plans cover. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to confirm your coverage.

faqs.q: How do I know if a skilled nursing facility is good?
faqs.a: Check its Medicare star rating on Care Compare, focusing on staffing and inspection scores, and ask about therapy hours, rehab-team experience, and discharge planning. Tour in person if possible and confirm it offers the specific therapy you need. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for help.

page.metaTitle: Medicaid Spend Down for Nursing Home | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Learn how Medicaid spend-down works to qualify for nursing home coverage — what counts, what's exempt, and the 5-year look-back. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: Medicaid Spend Down for Nursing Home Care
page.quickAnswer: A Medicaid spend-down is the legal process of reducing countable assets to meet Medicaid's limit (about $2,000 for an individual) so nursing home care is covered. You can spend on care, debts, home repairs, or exempt assets — but gifting triggers the 5-year look-back penalty. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free guidance.
page.heroSubhead: Spend-down can make long-term nursing home care affordable through Medicaid. Learn how to do it right — with free help from 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: If you have too many assets to qualify for Medicaid, a spend-down can help — but it must be done legally. Here's how it works.

sections.h2: What is a Medicaid spend-down?
sections.body: A Medicaid spend-down is the process of reducing your countable assets down to your state's limit — usually around $2,000 for an individual — so you become financially eligible for Medicaid nursing home coverage. Crucially, spend-down means spending on permitted things, not giving money away. Allowed uses include paying for care and medical bills, paying off a mortgage or other debts, making home repairs or modifications, buying a more reliable vehicle, prepaying funeral and burial expenses, and converting countable assets into exempt ones. Done correctly and early, a spend-down can preserve a home and protect a spouse while qualifying you for coverage. Mistakes can delay eligibility for months, so guidance matters. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: How does the look-back period affect spend-down?
sections.body: Medicaid's 5-year (60-month) look-back reviews asset transfers before your application. The key rule: spending down on yourself is fine, but gifting assets or selling them below market value can trigger a penalty period during which Medicaid won't pay. So paying your own bills, fixing your home, or buying exempt items is allowed; transferring money to children to 'hide' it is not — and can backfire badly. This is why spend-down should be planned early and carefully, ideally with an elder-law attorney, especially when a home or spouse is involved. The wrong move can cost months of coverage. Our specialists can point you to the right resources free of charge. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: What is a Medicaid spend-down for nursing home care?
faqs.a: It's the legal process of reducing countable assets to Medicaid's limit (about $2,000 for an individual) so nursing home care is covered. You spend on care, debts, home repairs, or exempt assets — not gifts. Done early and correctly, it preserves eligibility. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for guidance.

faqs.q: What can I spend money on during a Medicaid spend-down?
faqs.a: Permitted spending includes paying for care and medical bills, paying off debts and a mortgage, home repairs and modifications, a reliable vehicle, prepaid funeral and burial costs, and converting countable assets into exempt ones. Gifting assets is not allowed and triggers penalties. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help.

faqs.q: Can I give money to my children to qualify for Medicaid?
faqs.a: No. Gifting assets or selling them below market value within Medicaid's 5-year look-back triggers a penalty period during which Medicaid won't pay for care. Legitimate spend-down means spending on yourself, not transferring assets away. Plan early. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for guidance.

faqs.q: How long does a Medicaid spend-down take?
faqs.a: It depends on your assets and state rules. Some families spend down quickly on care costs; others plan over months or years to protect a home and spouse. Because the 5-year look-back penalizes gifts, early planning is best. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to get started.

faqs.q: Should I hire an attorney for a Medicaid spend-down?
faqs.a: For larger estates, a home, or a spouse, an elder-law attorney can prevent costly mistakes and protect assets legally. Simple cases may not require one. 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you understand your situation and point you to resources at no cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

children.keyword: nursing home vs skilled nursing facility

page.metaTitle: Nursing Home vs Skilled Nursing Facility | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: Nursing home vs skilled nursing facility: one is long-term custodial care, the other short-term medical rehab. See the difference and who pays. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.h1: Nursing Home vs. Skilled Nursing Facility
page.quickAnswer: A skilled nursing facility (SNF) provides short-term, medically intensive care like rehab after a hospital stay and is often covered by Medicare. A nursing home provides long-term custodial care for residents who live there indefinitely, paid by Medicaid or private funds. Many facilities do both. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help.
page.heroSubhead: The difference between a nursing home and skilled nursing facility affects your care and your costs. Get free clarity from 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: People use 'nursing home' and 'skilled nursing facility' interchangeably, but they mean different things — especially for payment. Here's the difference.

sections.h2: What is the difference between a nursing home and a skilled nursing facility?
sections.body: The core difference is purpose and duration. A skilled nursing facility (SNF) delivers short-term, medically intensive care — physical and occupational therapy, wound care, IV antibiotics — typically to help someone recover after a hospital stay and return home. A nursing home provides long-term custodial care: help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, and eating for residents who live there indefinitely. Confusingly, many facilities are dual-certified and offer both levels under one roof, which is why the terms blur. The distinction matters most for payment: Medicare covers skilled care, but not long-term custodial care. Knowing which level applies determines who pays. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help sorting it out.

sections.h2: Who pays for each type of care?
sections.body: Payment differs sharply. For skilled nursing facility care, Medicare covers up to 100 days per benefit period after a qualifying hospital stay — days 1-20 fully, days 21-100 with a coinsurance that Medigap can cover. For long-term nursing home (custodial) care, Medicare pays nothing; you rely on Medicaid (for those who qualify), long-term care insurance, or private funds. This is the single most important thing families get wrong: assuming Medicare will pay for an indefinite nursing home stay. It won't. Understanding which level of care your loved one needs — and how it's funded — can save tens of thousands of dollars and prevent painful surprises. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for free guidance.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a nursing home and a skilled nursing facility?
faqs.a: A skilled nursing facility provides short-term, medically intensive care like rehab after a hospital stay, often covered by Medicare. A nursing home provides long-term custodial care for residents living there indefinitely, paid by Medicaid or private funds. Many facilities offer both. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: Is a nursing home the same as a skilled nursing facility?
faqs.a: Not exactly, though the terms overlap. Skilled nursing is short-term medical/rehab care; a nursing home is long-term custodial care. Many facilities are dual-certified and do both, which is why people use the names interchangeably. The difference matters most for who pays. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for clarity.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover both nursing homes and skilled nursing facilities?
faqs.a: Medicare covers skilled nursing facility care for up to 100 days after a qualifying hospital stay, but it does not cover long-term custodial nursing home care. Long-term stays are paid by Medicaid, insurance, or private funds. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to understand your coverage.

faqs.q: How do I know which type of care my loved one needs?
faqs.a: Skilled care suits short-term recovery after surgery or illness when daily therapy or skilled nursing is required. Long-term custodial care suits ongoing help with daily activities. A doctor's assessment determines the level. Knowing it affects both care and cost. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for help.

children.keyword: what is a skilled nursing facility

page.metaTitle: What Is a Skilled Nursing Facility? | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: A skilled nursing facility provides short-term medical and rehab care, often after a hospital stay, covered by Medicare up to 100 days. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.h1: What Is a Skilled Nursing Facility?
page.quickAnswer: A skilled nursing facility (SNF) is a licensed facility providing short-term, medically intensive care — physical therapy, wound care, IV medication, and skilled nursing — usually after a hospital stay to help patients recover. Medicare covers up to 100 days per benefit period after a qualifying 3-day hospital admission. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help.
page.heroSubhead: Understand what a skilled nursing facility is, what it provides, and how Medicare covers it — with free help from 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: A skilled nursing facility plays a key role in recovery after a hospital stay. Here's what it is and how it's covered.

sections.h2: What does a skilled nursing facility provide?
sections.body: A skilled nursing facility (SNF) provides medical care that must be performed by, or under the supervision of, licensed health professionals — registered nurses, physical and occupational therapists, and speech-language pathologists. Typical services include post-surgical rehabilitation, physical and occupational therapy, wound care, IV medications and injections, and monitoring of complex medical conditions. The goal is usually recovery and return home, not permanent residence. SNFs differ from long-term nursing homes, which focus on ongoing custodial help with daily living. Most people enter a skilled nursing facility directly after a hospital stay to regain strength and function. Understanding this care level helps you plan recovery and coverage. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free guidance.

sections.h2: How does Medicare cover a skilled nursing facility?
sections.body: Medicare Part A covers skilled nursing facility care when you have a qualifying inpatient hospital stay of at least three days and need daily skilled care related to it. Coverage runs up to 100 days per benefit period: Medicare pays in full for days 1-20, then charges a daily coinsurance ($209.50 in 2025) for days 21-100, after which you pay all costs. Most Medicare Supplement (Medigap) plans cover that days 21-100 coinsurance, eliminating the out-of-pocket cost. Medicare Advantage plans cover skilled nursing differently, often with their own copays and rules. Knowing your specific coverage before you need it prevents surprise bills. A licensed agent can review it free. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

faqs.q: What is a skilled nursing facility?
faqs.a: A skilled nursing facility (SNF) is a licensed facility providing short-term, medically intensive care — physical therapy, wound care, IV medication, and skilled nursing — usually after a hospital stay to help patients recover and return home. Medicare covers it for up to 100 days per benefit period. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: What services does a skilled nursing facility provide?
faqs.a: Skilled nursing facilities provide post-surgical rehabilitation, physical and occupational therapy, speech therapy, wound care, IV medications, and monitoring of complex conditions — all delivered by licensed health professionals. The goal is recovery and return home rather than permanent residence. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help understanding coverage.

faqs.q: How is a skilled nursing facility different from a nursing home?
faqs.a: A skilled nursing facility provides short-term medical and rehab care, often Medicare-covered. A nursing home provides long-term custodial care for residents living there indefinitely, paid by Medicaid or private funds. Many facilities are dual-certified and offer both. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for clarity.

faqs.q: Does Medicare pay for a skilled nursing facility?
faqs.a: Yes, with conditions. After a qualifying 3-day inpatient hospital stay, Medicare covers up to 100 days per benefit period — days 1-20 fully, days 21-100 with a daily coinsurance ($209.50 in 2025) that most Medigap plans cover. After 100 days you pay all costs. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: How long can you stay in a skilled nursing facility?
faqs.a: Medicare covers up to 100 days of skilled nursing care per benefit period, but coverage continues only while you need daily skilled care and are progressing. If skilled care is no longer needed, coverage may end sooner. You can stay longer by paying privately. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
